headlines.geopolitics: Community honors 'unclaimed' Navy veteran with full military burial
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting 'insane' Medicaid scams
headlines.domestic: Voters expect AI revolution but find it barely useful today

metadata.daily_do: Check in on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community this week

clips.caption: When Duke gets unexpectedly emotional about veterans 🇺🇸
Pringle explains why we honor unknown soldiers
Jax discovers the government is using AI to find office chairs

script.text: Two guys from Philly drove to Minneapolis, got signatures from homeless people, and walked away with four million dollars. And nobody cared.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March tenth. Here's what happened while you were sleeping.

First, geopolitics. In Nashville, hundreds gathered at the Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery to honor Navy veteran Lonnie Wayman. He was listed as 'unclaimed' after no family came forward, but the community refused to let him be buried alone.
Oh wow.

Veterans, community members, and military personnel filled the ceremony. A VA representative said quote, 'unclaimed does not mean forgotten,' and they provided full military honors including a dove release.
Good.

Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division targeting what he called the 'insane' problem of federal program fraud. The initiative will prosecute any fraud over one thousand dollars.
A thousand bucks? That's like, three grocery trips.

Blanche cited cases including two Pennsylvania men who fraudulently received four million dollars for fake Minneapolis homeless shelters. He said the federal government gives 'money away like candy' with insufficient oversight.
Four million? Jesus.
And domestic. A new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, but only forty-seven percent find it useful today.
Fascinating disconnect.
Eighty percent are very or extremely concerned that AI is eroding trust in news and social media. Eighty-nine percent want clear disclosure when content is AI-generated.
So we're afraid of the robot apocalypse but also can't figure out how to use ChatGPT for grocery lists.
That's the news. Let's talk about it.
I want to start with Lonnie Wayman's funeral because, um, this is one of those stories that cuts through everything else.
I saw the photos. The sanctuary was packed. People standing outside.
What struck me was the VA representative saying 'unclaimed' was the wrong word. That it was a misnomer.
Because he wasn't unclaimed. The whole damn community claimed him.
Which raises the question of how many other veterans are in this situation. Like, how did we get to a place where a Navy veteran dies alone?
The VA doesn't track comprehensive data on unclaimed veterans, but estimates suggest it's in the thousands annually.
Wait, thousands?
Look, this isn't just about veterans. This is about what happens when communities fall apart. When people don't know their neighbors.
But Duke, this is also a policy failure. We have programs for veteran benefits, veteran healthcare, but apparently not for making sure veterans don't die alone and forgotten.
Programs don't replace people, Bree. You can't bureaucratize caring about somebody.
I'm not saying bureaucratize it, I'm saying we need systems that connect isolated veterans with resources and community. Social workers, case managers, volunteer networks.
We had that in Nashville. It was called neighbors showing up to a funeral.
But Duke, that happened after he died. The question is prevention.
Fair point. Look, I'm not against programs. I just think the answer isn't more federal oversight. It's communities giving a damn.
Why can't it be both? Community caring plus structural support?
Can I ask something? How do you even end up with no family? Like, none at all?
Military service can be isolating. Frequent moves, deployments. Some veterans struggle with reintegration, PTSD, substance abuse. Relationships fracture.
And some guys just keep to themselves. That's not a character flaw.
Which is exactly why we need proactive outreach. Wellness checks, social services that actually work.
Or we need to be the kind of people who check on the guy next door.
What's interesting is this wasn't a government program that organized the funeral. It was grassroots. Veterans groups, community members, the mortuary.
So the system failed him in life but the community caught him in death.
That's one way to put it. Another way is that people still know right from wrong.
I don't disagree with that. But I also think we can prevent these situations without waiting for someone to die.
The broader question is what we owe people who served. And what that obligation looks like in practice.
We owe them everything. But that debt isn't paid by the VA. It's paid by us. By showing up.
And that's beautiful, Duke. But beautiful isn't scalable. We need both the human touch and the institutional support.
What happened in Nashville was remarkable. But it shouldn't be remarkable. It should be normal.
Exactly. And the fact that it made national news tells you everything about where we are as a country.
I keep thinking about that phrase. 'Unclaimed.' Like he was a piece of luggage at the airport.
Which is why the VA representative corrected it. Language matters.
It does. And so do the systems that create that language in the first place.
Look, at the end of the day, Lonnie Wayman got the send-off he deserved. That matters.
It does. And maybe that's the story. Not the system failure, but the community response.
If I may interject, Leo, this reminds me of something.
Please, Professor.

The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. Established in 1921 at Arlington National Cemetery. The idea came from a British chaplain named David Railton, who saw a grave in France marked simply 'An Unknown British Soldier.'
The concept spread across the world after the Great War. Nations realized they had thousands of unidentified dead, and rather than let them be forgotten, they chose one to represent all.
That's beautiful.

Here's what's remarkable about the American tomb. When they selected the unknown soldier in 1921, Sergeant Edward Younger chose from four caskets. He placed white roses on one. That soldier was brought to Arlington with full honors.
But here's the key detail. The ceremony wasn't organized by bureaucrats. It was driven by mothers who had lost sons, veterans who had served, communities that refused to let service go unrecognized.
Same impulse as Nashville.
Precisely. The tomb represents every unknown soldier, but the idea came from people who said 'this cannot stand.' Not from policy, from conscience.
Today, three soldiers guard that tomb twenty-four hours a day, every day of the year. Through hurricanes, blizzards, everything. They've done it for over a century.
Wait, really? Even during like, hurricanes?
Hurricane Isabel in 2003. The military offered to suspend the guard. The sentinels refused. They said the unknown soldiers had already given everything. The least they could do was stand watch.
Oh my god.

That's the parallel to Nashville. Lonnie Wayman was unknown to most of those people, but they understood something fundamental. Service deserves recognition. Sacrifice deserves honor. And no one who served should be forgotten.
The difference being the tomb was institutionalized. It became permanent.

Because the impulse was so strong, so universal, that it demanded permanence. Perhaps that's what we're seeing in Nashville. Not just a one-time response, but a recognition that this should be how we do things.
That's exactly right. This wasn't charity. This was duty.
And duty, once recognized, tends to become tradition. I suspect Nashville will not be the last city to refuse to let a veteran be buried alone.
Professor, that's like, genuinely moving. I wasn't expecting to get emotional about military history at nine in the morning.
History is emotional, Jax. It's about people making choices under pressure. And sometimes, thank goodness, they choose well.
Alright, let's shift gears before I start crying. Time for Rapid Fire, where the news gets weird and I get to make fun of it.
First up, artificial intelligence has officially reached peak government efficiency. Palantir's AI weaponry is now being deployed to find office chairs for federal staff.
I'm sorry, what?
Apparently the same technology they use to track terrorists is now optimizing office furniture procurement. Because nothing says 'national security' like ergonomic lumbar support.
That's the most government thing I've ever heard.
I love that somewhere in a bunker, there's a guy staring at a screen that says 'Herman Miller chair detected, threat level: beige.'
Moving on.
In workplace harassment news, an Irish employee won a case against his boss who repeatedly shouted 'potato' at him. Which is either deeply offensive or the world's most specific Tourette's syndrome.
That's horrible.
The boss's defense was apparently that he shouts random food items at everyone. Which, I don't know, 'BROCCOLI!' doesn't sound much better as a management strategy.
How do you even explain that in court? 'Your honor, my client has a vegetable-based communication disorder.'

Louisiana classrooms are now required to post the Ten Commandments by Friday. Which means thousands of teachers spent their weekend at Staples buying poster board like it's a middle school science fair.
The Supreme Court will find this fascinating.
I'm imagining some poor art teacher trying to make them aesthetically pleasing. Like, 'Thou shalt not kill' in Comic Sans with little flowers around it.
Stop, that's exactly what's happening right now.
And finally, a Florida drug dealer locked a man in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt. Which is either criminal imprisonment or the world's worst Airbnb experience.
That's kidnapping, Jax.

Oh absolutely. But also, Florida man continues to Florida. The guy was discovered when someone heard him banging on the unit door. Imagine being the person who finds that. 'Honey, I think our storage unit is haunted.' 'No, that's just Steve. He owes money.'
How do you even explain that to the paramedics?
Right? 'What happened?' 'Well, I bought some drugs, didn't pay for them, and now I live in unit forty-seven next to someone's Christmas decorations.'
The rent was probably still cheaper than Miami Beach.
Oh snap, Bree with the real estate burn. I see you.
Alright, let's bring it home. Today's Daily Do comes directly from Lonnie Wayman's story.
Check in on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community this week. Not because you have to, but because it matters. A phone call, a knock on the door, a conversation at the mailbox.
What happened in Nashville was remarkable, but it was also reactive. We can be proactive. We can make sure fewer people feel forgotten before it's too late.
That's exactly right, Leo. And it doesn't take a program or a policy. It just takes giving a damn.
And if you find someone who needs more support than you can provide, that's when you call the professionals. But start with showing up.
Professor, any final thoughts?
Edmund Burke once said, 'The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing.' Today I'd modify that slightly.
The only thing necessary for loneliness to triumph is for neighbors to do nothing. Nashville chose differently. So can we.
That's The Friction for Monday, March tenth. I'm Jax Mooney reminding you that community isn't built by committees, it's built by showing up. We'll see you tomorrow.
